top_papers.title: Numerical Superoptimization for Library Learning
top_papers.summary:
Numerical software depends on fast, accurate implementations of mathematical primitives like sin, exp, and log. Modern superoptimizers can optimize floating-point kernels against a given set of such primitives, but a more fundamental question remains open: which new primitives are worth implementing in the first place?
  We formulate this as numerical library learning: given a workload of floating-point kernels, identify the mathematical primitives whose expert implementations would most improve speed and accuracy. Our key insight is that numerical superoptimizers already have the machinery well-suited to this problem. Their search procedures happen to enumerate candidate primitives, their equivalence procedures can generalize and deduplicate candidates, and their cost models can estimate counterfactual utility: how much the workload would improve if a given primitive were available.
  We present GrowLibm, which repurposes the Herbie superoptimizer as a numerical library learner. GrowLibm mines candidate primitives from the superoptimizer's intermediate search results, ranks them by counterfactual utility, and prunes redundant candidates. Across three scientific applications (PROJ, CoolProp, and Basilisk), GrowLibm identifies compact, reusable primitives that can be implemented effectively using standard numerical techniques. When Herbie is extended with these expert implementations, kernel speed improves by up to 2.2x at fixed accuracy, and maximum achievable accuracy also improves, in one case from 56.0% to 93.5%. We also prototype an LLVM matcher that recognizes learned primitives in optimized IR, recovering 26 replacement sites across five PROJ projections and improving end-to-end application performance by up to 5%.
top_papers.matched_domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

match_details.domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

ai.one_liner_zh: This paper reframes numerical library design as a search problem: mine candidate math primitives from superoptimization traces, rank them by counterfactual workload utility, and add only the primitives that materially improve downstream kernels.
ai.summary_zh: This paper studies a more upstream question than kernel-level numerical optimization: not just how to optimize floating-point code given a math library, but which new primitives should exist in that library at all. It introduces numerical library learning and implements it in GrowLibm, which repurposes Herbie's search, equivalence handling, and cost modeling to mine, deduplicate, and rank candidate primitives against real workloads. The abstract reports that adding expert implementations of the learned primitives improves kernel speed by up to 2.2x at fixed accuracy, can raise achievable accuracy substantially in at least one case, and can also be recovered from optimized LLVM IR to deliver up to 5% end-to-end application gains. The practical value is a workflow for expanding numerical libraries in a workload-driven way instead of relying only on manual expert intuition. The main missing detail from the abstract is how robust the ranking, implementation effort, and benchmark fairness are across broader workloads and hardware/compiler settings.
ai.background_zh: Numerical kernels depend heavily on a small set of expertly implemented math primitives, but deciding which additional primitives deserve that expert engineering effort has largely remained ad hoc.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to identify new reusable mathematical primitives whose addition to a numerical library would most improve the speed and accuracy of a target workload.
ai.approach_zh: GrowLibm reuses a numerical superoptimizer's existing search outputs, equivalence machinery, and cost model to extract candidate primitives, estimate their counterfactual utility, and prune redundant options before implementation.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract claims results on three scientific applications plus an LLVM-based recovery prototype, with reported kernel-level speed and accuracy improvements and smaller end-to-end gains, but it does not fully specify evaluation methodology in the abstract.
ai.value_zh: If the method holds up, it offers a principled way to co-design numerical libraries and compiler recognition around workload value rather than only hand-chosen primitives.
open_questions: How is counterfactual utility estimated, and how sensitive is the ranking to the workload mix, cost model, and search budget?
What implementation effort is required to turn a learned primitive into a stable expert routine, and how often do the learned candidates generalize beyond the training applications?
Are the reported speed and accuracy gains measured against strong numerical and compiler baselines under comparable optimization settings, including the LLVM matcher path?
core_contributions: Formulates numerical library learning as the problem of selecting new math primitives for a workload rather than only optimizing kernels under a fixed library.
Builds GrowLibm by repurposing Herbie's search traces, equivalence reasoning, and cost modeling to mine and rank candidate primitives.
Shows a compiler-facing prototype that matches learned primitives in optimized LLVM IR and recovers some end-to-end application benefit.
why_read: It shifts attention from local floating-point rewrites to the library-design layer, which is a more structural optimization target.
It provides a concrete mechanism for connecting superoptimization search artifacts to reusable primitive discovery.
The LLVM matcher angle makes the work relevant to compiler/runtime co-design rather than pure numerical analysis alone.
risks: The abstract gives strong headline gains, but the breadth of workloads is narrow and may not reflect general-purpose numerical software.
The usefulness of the learned primitives may depend on substantial expert implementation work that the abstract does not quantify clearly.
recommended_for: Researchers working on numerical optimization, superoptimization, and compiler support for floating-point kernels.
Engineers interested in workload-driven math library design and IR-level recognition of higher-level numerical idioms.
Readers tracking systems-adjacent program synthesis ideas that may influence low-level code generation pipelines.
ai.reading_priority_reason: The paper is technically interesting and methodologically original, especially for compiler and low-level optimization readers, but it is only indirectly aligned with the stated LLM inference and CUDA-kernel focus and leaves important evaluation details to the full paper.
ai.fallback_summary: Numerical software depends on fast, accurate implementations of mathematical primitives like sin, exp, and log.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are factual reliability and verification. The set is anchored by Numerical Superoptimization for….
reading_strategy: Start with Numerical Superoptimization for… and verify whether it defines the factual reliability and verification problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
